JUb19 as donor versus all other recipients

Data formating from pairwise competition

Compile data from different competition assays (JUb19 as donor and other species as recipient) at start (0 hr) and end (24 hr) points (under OD = 1).

Frequency lineplot (JUb19 vs others)

Here, as a first attempt to visualize the competitive outcome, we plot the frequency change across 0 and 24 hrs. The first figure shows the frequency change for JUb19 (donor) and the second mirrored figure shows that for the other species (recipient).

Interspecies competition value between donor and recepient (JUb19 vs others)

Again, consider a Lotkta–Volterra competition model in the form of \(\frac{dN_i}{dt} = N_i(r_i-\alpha_{ii}N_i-\alpha_{ij}N_j)\). While we used a statistical fitting approach to estimate the intraspecific competition coefficients (\(\alpha_{ii}\)H), we cannot do that for the interspecific competition coefficients (\(\alpha_{ij}\)) due to the lack of a density gradient (but see the results for Jub19 and CEN2ent in a separate file). Instead, we use a “hybrid” approach. Here, in the Lotka–Volterra competition model, the per capita growth rate is still estimated as \(\frac{\Delta lnN_i}{\Delta t}\), and the species’ \(r_i\) and \(\alpha_{ii}\) are obtained by the previous statistical fitting results for the monoculture growth assay (i.e., \(\hat{r_i}\) and \(\hat{\alpha_{ii}}\)). By rearranging the equation, we can indirectly obtain an esitmate for the interspecific competition coefficients as follows:
\[\alpha_{DR} = \left[\hat{r_D}-\hat{\alpha_{DD}}N_{D0}^c-\left(\frac{lnN_{D24}^c - lnN_{D0}^c}{24}\right)\right].\left(\frac{1}{N_{R0}^c}\right)\] \[\alpha_{RD} = \left[\hat{r_R}-\hat{\alpha_{RR}}N_{R0}^c-\left(\frac{lnN_{R24}^c - lnN_{R0}^c}{24}\right)\right].\left(\frac{1}{N_{D0}^c}\right)\], where \(N_{jt}^c\) represents the scaled population size of species \(j\) (= \(D\) of \(R\)) at time \(t\) (= 0 or 24 hr) within the co-culture (superscript \(c\)). Note again all density data were scaled by \(10^9\) prior to calculation. Below, we show (left) the estimated effect of other recipient species of JUb19 (\(\alpha_{DR}\)), and (right) the estimated effect of JUb19 on the other recipient species (\(\alpha_{RD}\), with \(D\) = JUb19). Indeed, most species are heavily impacted by JUb19 except CEN2ent.
